result.markdown:
# Loading and Exploring the Data ## Learning Objectives After completing this chapter, you should be able to - load one WISDM phone accelerometer file, - load all participant files into one DataFrame, - map activity codes to readable activity names, - restrict the dataset to selected target activities, - inspect class distribution and subject distribution, - compute and visualize acceleration magnitude, - estimate the sampling rate from timestamps. --- ## Why This Chapter Matters Before we create windows, extract features, or train a model, we need to make sure that we can load and understand the raw data. This chapter turns a folder of raw text files into a structured table that we can analyze and process further. ::: {.callout-tip} ## Engineering Insight A clean data loading step is one of the most important parts of a reproducible machine learning project. If the loading logic is unclear or inconsistent, every later result becomes questionable. ::: --- ## Step 1 — Import Required Libraries ### Goal We import the libraries needed for data loading, numerical processing and visualization. ::: {.cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} from pathlib import Path import sys import numpy as np import pandas as pd import matplotlib.pyplot as plt ``` ::: ### Expected Output This cell should run without errors. --- ## Step 2 — Configure Project Paths ### Goal We define where the project root and the data directory are located. ::: {.cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} current_path = Path.cwd().resolve() for parent in [current_path] + list(current_path.parents): if (parent / "app").exists(): PROJECT_ROOT = parent break else: raise RuntimeError("Could not find project root containing app/ directory.") sys.path.insert(0, str(PROJECT_ROOT)) from app.config import DATA_DIR from app.data_loader import ( ACTIVITY_MAP, TARGET_ACTIVITIES, load_single_wisdm_file, load_wisdm_phone_accel, ) from app.pipeline import add_magnitude print("Project root:", PROJECT_ROOT) print("Data directory:", DATA_DIR) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Project root: /Users/konak/HPI/Vorlesungen/Data Engineering for Wearables SS2026/Mini-Project/de4w-engineering-workbook Data directory: /Users/konak/HPI/Vorlesungen/Data Engineering for Wearables SS2026/Mini-Project/de4w-engineering-workbook/data ``` ::: ::: ### Expected Output The output should point to your project folder and the `data/` directory. ::: {.callout-warning} ## Common Pitfall If the printed path does not point to your project folder, you may have started Quarto from the wrong directory. ::: --- ## Step 3 — Define Activity Mapping ### Goal The raw WISDM files use activity codes such as `A`, `D`, and `E`. We map these codes to readable names. ::: {.cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} ACTIVITY_MAP ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=3} ``` {'A': 'walking', 'B': 'jogging', 'C': 'stairs', 'D': 'sitting', 'E': 'standing', 'F': 'typing', 'G': 'brushing_teeth', 'H': 'eating_soup', 'I': 'eating_chips', 'J': 'eating_pasta', 'K': 'drinking', 'L': 'eating_sandwich', 'M': 'kicking', 'O': 'catch', 'P': 'dribbling', 'Q': 'writing', 'R': 'clapping', 'S': 'folding_clothes'} ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} TARGET_ACTIVITIES ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=4} ``` ['walking', 'sitting', 'standing'] ``` ::: ::: ### Discussion We keep the complete mapping even though we currently use only three activities. This makes it easier to extend the project later. --- ## Step 4 — Inspect Available Files ### Goal Before loading the data, we check which raw files are available. ::: {.cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} files = sorted(DATA_DIR.glob("data_*_accel_phone.txt")) len(files), files[:5] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=5} ``` (51, [PosixPath('/Users/konak/HPI/Vorlesungen/Data Engineering for Wearables SS2026/Mini-Project/de4w-engineering-workbook/data/data_1600_accel_phone.txt'), PosixPath('/Users/konak/HPI/Vorlesungen/Data Engineering for Wearables SS2026/Mini-Project/de4w-engineering-workbook/data/data_1601_accel_phone.txt'), PosixPath('/Users/konak/HPI/Vorlesungen/Data Engineering for Wearables SS2026/Mini-Project/de4w-engineering-workbook/data/data_1602_accel_phone.txt'), PosixPath('/Users/konak/HPI/Vorlesungen/Data Engineering for Wearables SS2026/Mini-Project/de4w-engineering-workbook/data/data_1603_accel_phone.txt'), PosixPath('/Users/konak/HPI/Vorlesungen/Data Engineering for Wearables SS2026/Mini-Project/de4w-engineering-workbook/data/data_1604_accel_phone.txt')]) ``` ::: ::: ### Expected Output You should see approximately 51 files, one per participant. --- ## Step 5 — Load One Participant File ### Goal We first load a single file to understand the raw structure. ::: {.cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} load_single_wisdm_file ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=6} ``` pandas.DataFrame> ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code} df_one = load_single_wisdm_file(files[0]) df_one.head() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=7} ```{=html}
subject_id activity_code timestamp x y z activity
0 1600 A 252207666810782 -0.364761 8.793503 1.055084 walking
1 1600 A 252207717164786 -0.879730 9.768784 1.016998 walking
2 1600 A 252207767518790 2.001495 11.109070 2.619156 walking
3 1600 A 252207817872794 0.450623 12.651642 0.184555 walking
4 1600 A 252207868226798 -2.164352 13.928436 -4.422485 walking
``` ::: ::: ### Expected Output You should see a table with the columns: ```text subject_id, activity_code, timestamp, x, y, z, activity ``` --- ## Step 6 — Load All Participant Files ### Goal We now load all phone accelerometer files and combine them into a single DataFrame. ::: {.cell execution_count=8} ``` {.python .cell-code} load_wisdm_phone_accel ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=8} ``` pandas.DataFrame> ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell execution_count=9} ``` {.python .cell-code} df = load_wisdm_phone_accel( DATA_DIR, target_activities=TARGET_ACTIVITIES, ) df.head() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=9} ```{=html}
subject_id activity_code timestamp x y z activity
0 1600 A 252207666810782 -0.364761 8.793503 1.055084 walking
1 1600 A 252207717164786 -0.879730 9.768784 1.016998 walking
2 1600 A 252207767518790 2.001495 11.109070 2.619156 walking
3 1600 A 252207817872794 0.450623 12.651642 0.184555 walking
4 1600 A 252207868226798 -2.164352 13.928436 -4.422485 walking
``` ::: ::: --- ## Step 7 — Inspect the Dataset ### Goal We check the size, classes and number of subjects. ::: {.cell execution_count=10} ``` {.python .cell-code} df.shape ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=10} ``` (814013, 7) ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell execution_count=11} ``` {.python .cell-code} df["activity"].value_counts() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=11} ``` activity walking 279817 standing 269604 sitting 264592 Name: count, dtype: int64 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell execution_count=12} ``` {.python .cell-code} df["subject_id"].nunique() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=12} ``` 51 ``` ::: ::: ### Discussion At this point, we should verify that - all expected subjects were loaded, - all target activities are present, - the class distribution is not extremely imbalanced. --- ## Step 8 — Add Acceleration Magnitude ### Goal We compute the magnitude of the three-dimensional acceleration vector. $$ m = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2} $$ ::: {.cell execution_count=13} ``` {.python .cell-code} df = add_magnitude(df) df.head() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=13} ```{=html}
subject_id activity_code timestamp x y z activity magnitude
0 1600 A 252207666810782 -0.364761 8.793503 1.055084 walking 8.864082
1 1600 A 252207717164786 -0.879730 9.768784 1.016998 walking 9.860900
2 1600 A 252207767518790 2.001495 11.109070 2.619156 walking 11.587812
3 1600 A 252207817872794 0.450623 12.651642 0.184555 walking 12.661010
4 1600 A 252207868226798 -2.164352 13.928436 -4.422485 walking 14.773088
``` ::: ::: ### Discussion The magnitude combines all three axes into one signal. It is often easier to interpret than individual axes because it is less sensitive to phone orientation. --- ## Step 9 — Plot One Activity ### Goal We visualize x, y, z and magnitude for one activity. ::: {.cell execution_count=14} ``` {.python .cell-code} activity = "walking" sample = ( df[df["activity"] == activity] .head(400) .reset_index(drop=True) ) plt.figure(figsize=(12, 4)) plt.plot(sample["x"], label="x") plt.plot(sample["y"], label="y") plt.plot(sample["z"], label="z") plt.plot(sample["magnitude"], label="magnitude", linewidth=2) plt.title(f"Phone accelerometer signal: {activity}") plt.xlabel("Sample") plt.ylabel("Acceleration [m/s²]") plt.legend() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](03_loading_and_exploration_files/figure-pdf/cell-15-output-1.pdf){fig-pos='H'} ::: ::: ### Discussion Walking usually produces a more dynamic and periodic signal than sitting or standing. --- ## Step 10 — Compare Activities ### Goal We compare the magnitude signal for walking, sitting and standing. ::: {.cell execution_count=15} ``` {.python .cell-code} plt.figure(figsize=(12, 5)) for activity in TARGET_ACTIVITIES: signal = ( df[df["activity"] == activity]["magnitude"] .head(400) .reset_index(drop=True) ) plt.plot(signal, label=activity) plt.title("Magnitude comparison") plt.xlabel("Sample") plt.ylabel("Magnitude") plt.legend() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](03_loading_and_exploration_files/figure-pdf/cell-16-output-1.pdf){fig-pos='H'} ::: ::: ### Discussion Walking should usually be easier to distinguish because it contains stronger and more regular movement. Sitting and standing are more similar and may be harder to separate. --- ## Step 11 — Estimate the Sampling Rate ### Goal The WISDM documentation states that the data was collected at approximately 20 Hz. We estimate the sampling rate from timestamps. ::: {.cell execution_count=16} ``` {.python .cell-code} subject = df["subject_id"].iloc[0] activity = "walking" sample = df[ (df["subject_id"] == subject) & (df["activity"] == activity) ].sort_values("timestamp") dt = sample["timestamp"].diff().dropna() sampling_rate = 1e9 / dt.mean() sampling_rate ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=16} ``` np.float64(19.85939378331699) ``` ::: ::: ### Expected Output The result should be close to 20 Hz. ::: {.callout-note} ## Remember Documentation is important, but data engineers should still verify assumptions directly from the data. ::: --- ## Checkpoint Before continuing, you should be able to answer: 1. Why do we load one file before loading all files? 2. Why do we keep the subject identifier? 3. What is acceleration magnitude? 4. Why are sitting and standing harder to distinguish than walking? 5. Why is the sampling rate important for the next chapter? --- ## Further Exploration Optional: modify the target activity selection so that the dataset also includes `jogging`. Which parts of the pipeline would change? --- ## Summary In this chapter you learned how to - load one raw WISDM file, - load all participant files, - map activity codes, - inspect class and subject distributions, - compute acceleration magnitude, - visualize raw signals, - estimate sampling rate. In the next chapter we will transform the continuous sensor stream into fixed-size windows.
